svg.text: Near-threshold chemistry: amplitude settles slower than period
The Hopf boundary is exact. The newborn cycle size is not. Short settle windows overstate amplitude long before they noticeably move the period.
short horizon
long horizon
Brusselator x-amplitude near B = 1 + A²
A = 1.00; short and long horizons tell different stories right after onset
ΔB above the Hopf curve
At ΔB=0.01, the short horizon adds 0.11 of extra x-amplitude; by ΔB=0.28, the gap is -0.00.
Selkov x-amplitude just inside the lower Hopf edge
a = 0.08; the same finite-time bias shows up inside the oscillatory band
Δb above the lower Hopf edge
At Δb=0.003, the short horizon adds 0.04 of extra x-amplitude; by Δb=0.16, the gap is -0.00.
Brusselator period is already stable
The two period curves almost overlap while amplitude is still settling
ΔB above the Hopf curve
Maximum period gap across both models in this card: 0.060 time units.
Selkov period settles before amplitude does
Near threshold, period shifts a little while amplitude shifts much more
Δb above the lower Hopf edge
A single fixed scan can locate onset before it can trust the newborn cycle size.
Read the card like this
• Brusselator: at ΔB=0.01, x-amplitude falls from 0.24 on the short horizon to 0.13 on the long one, while period only shifts from 6.30 to 6.29.
• Selkov: at Δb=0.003, x-amplitude falls from 0.11 to 0.07, with period moving only from 14.08 to 14.02.
• The threshold tells you where oscillation begins. The finite cycle size near that threshold still needs enough settling time.
